### The jemalloc Connection
***

Virtually all modern allocators allocate memory in fixed-size
quanta that are chosen to minimize management overhead while at
the same time offering good coverage at low slack. For example, an
allocator may choose blocks of doubling size (32, 64, 128,
<t_co>, ...) up to 4096, and then blocks of size multiples of a
page up until 1MB, and then 512KB increments and so on.

As discussed above, `std::vector` also needs to (re)allocate in
quanta. The next quantum is usually defined in terms of the
current size times the infamous growth constant. Because of this
setup, `std::vector` has some slack memory at the end much like
an allocated block has some slack memory at the end.

It doesn't take a rocket surgeon to figure out that an allocator-
aware `std::vector` would be a marriage made in heaven: the
vector could directly request blocks of "perfect" size from the
allocator so there would be virtually no slack in the allocator.
Also, the entire growth strategy could be adjusted to work
perfectly with allocator's own block growth strategy. That's
exactly what `fbvector` does - it automatically detects the use
of jemalloc and adjusts its reallocation strategy accordingly.

But wait, there's more. Many memory allocators do not support in-
place reallocation, although most of them could. This comes from
the now notorious design of `realloc()` to opaquely perform
either in-place reallocation or an allocate-memcpy-deallocate
cycle. Such lack of control subsequently forced all clib-based
allocator designs to avoid in-place reallocation, and that
includes C++'s `new` and `std::allocator`. This is a major loss of
efficiency because an in-place reallocation, being very cheap,
may mean a much less aggressive growth strategy. In turn that
means less slack

### Implementation
***

AtomicHashMap is a composition of AtomicHashArray submaps, which implement the
meat of the functionality.  Only one AHA is created on initialization, and
additional submaps are appended if the first one gets full.  If the AHM grows,
there will be multiple submaps that must be probed in series to find a given
key.  The more growth, the more submaps will be chained, and the slower it will
get.  If the initial size estimate is good, only one submap will ever be created
and performance will be optimal.

AtomicHashArray is a fixed-size probing hash map (also referred to as an open
addressed hash map) where hash collisions are resolved by checking subsequent
elements.  This means that they can be allocated in slabs as arrays of
value_type elements, have excellent cache performance, and have no memory
overhead from storing pointers.

The algorithm is simple - when inserting, the key is hash-mod'ed to an offset,
and that element-key is atomically compare-and-swap'ed with the locked key
value.  If successful, the value is written and the element-key is unlocked by
setting it to the input key value.  If the compare fails, the next element is
tried until success or the map is full.

Finds are even simpler.  The key is hash-mod'ed to an offset, and the
element-key is examined.  If it is the same as the input key, the reference is
returned, if it's the empty key, failure is returned, otherwise the next key is
tried.  This can be done wait-free without any atomic instructions because the
elements are always in a valid state.

Erase is done by finding the key, then compare-and-swap'ing the element-key with
the reserved erased key value.  If the swap succeeds, return success, otherwise
return failure (the element was erased by a competing thread).  If the key do

### Motivation
***

`std::vector` is the stalwart abstraction many use for
dynamically-allocated arrays in C++. It is also the best known
and most used of all containers. It may therefore seem a
surprise that `std::vector` leaves important - and sometimes
vital - efficiency opportunities on the table. This document
explains how our own drop-in abstraction `fbvector` improves key
performance aspects of `std::vector`. Refer to
folly/test/FBVectorTest.cpp for a few benchmarks.

### Limitations
***

Although it can provide extreme performance, AtomicHashmap has some unique
limitations as well.

* The space for erased elements cannot be reclaimed (they are tombstoned
forever) so it's generally not a good idea to use this if you're erasing things
a lot.

* Only supports 32 or 64 bit keys - this is because they must be atomically
compare-and-swap'ed.

* Growth beyond initialization reduces performance - if you don't know
the approximate number of elements you'll be inserting into the map, you
probably shouldn't use this class.

* Must manage synchronization externally in order to modify values in the map
after insertion.  Lock pools are a common way to do this, or you may
consider using `folly::PackedSyncPtr<T>` as your `ValueT`.

* Must define special reserved key values for empty, erased, and locked
elements.

For a complete list of limitations and departures from the
UnorderedAssociativeContainer concept, see `folly/AtomicHashMap.h`

tests.title: Explain benchmark State loop semantics
tests.prompt: Explain what the benchmark::State loop and DoNotOptimize are doing.
tests.notes: Retrieval precheck failed: Expected file fragment missing.
tests.response:
- `benchmark::State& state` is a parameter passed to benchmark functions to provide access to runtime statistics and control loop execution.
- The `for (auto _ : state)` loop runs the function repeatedly, with `state.iterations()` indicating how many times it should execute.
- `benchmark::DoNotOptimize(iterations)` is called to prevent the compiler from optimizing out the loop or its contents, ensuring actual execution time is measured.
- Used to prevent aggressive code elimination or loop unrolling optimizations during benchmarking.

### Implementation
***

AtomicHashMap is a composition of AtomicHashArray submaps, which implement the
meat of the functionality.  Only one AHA is created on initialization, and
additional submaps are appended if the first one gets full.  If the AHM grows,
there will be multiple submaps that must be probed in series to find a given
key.  The more growth, the more submaps will be chained, and the slower it will
get.  If the initial size estimate is good, only one submap will ever be created
and performance will be optimal.

AtomicHashArray is a fixed-size probing hash map (also referred to as an open
addressed hash map) where hash collisions are resolved by checking subsequent
elements.  This means that they can be allocated in slabs as arrays of
value_type elements, have excellent cache performance, and have no memory
overhead from storing pointers.

The algorithm is simple - when inserting, the key is hash-mod'ed to an offset,
and that element-key is atomically compare-and-swap'ed with the locked key
value.  If successful, the value is written and the element-key is unlocked by
setting it to the input key value.  If the compare fails, the next element is
tried until success or the map is full.

Finds are even simpler.  The key is hash-mod'ed to an offset, and the
element-key is examined.  If it is the same as the input key, the reference is
returned, if it's the empty key, failure is returned, otherwise the next key is
tried.  This can be done wait-free without any atomic instructions because the
elements are always in a valid state.

Erase is done by finding the key, then compare-and-swap'ing the element-key with
the reserved erased key value.  If the swap succeeds, return success, otherwise
return failure (the element was erased by a competing thread).  If the key do

tests.title: Explain gtest fixture lifecycle
tests.prompt: Explain TEST_F fixtures and the role of SetUp and TearDown.
tests.response:
- TEST_F is a template macro that defines parameterized test fixtures, allowing shared test setup/teardown between test cases.  
- It takes a test fixture class (derived from `testing::Test`) and a test case name as arguments.  
- The macro automatically derives from `Test` and calls `SetUp()` and `TearDown()` before and after each test case.  
- `SetUp()` is called once per test suite to initialize shared resources for all tests.  
- `TearDown()` is called once per test suite to clean up shared resources after all tests.  
- `TEST_F` enables multiple test cases to reuse `SetUp()`/`TearDown()` without duplication.

tests.title: Explain ASSERT_EQ versus EXPECT_EQ
tests.prompt: Explain the behavior difference between ASSERT_EQ and EXPECT_EQ.
tests.notes: Retrieval precheck failed: Keyword hits 0 < 1.
tests.response:
- `EXPECT_EQ` is an assert-like test that **does not terminate** on failure; it records the failure and continues execution.  
- `ASSERT_EQ` is a debug assertion that **terminates immediately** with an error if the two values are not equal.  
- `EXPECT_EQ` uses `EXPECT_PRED_FORMAT2` with `::testing::internal::CmpHelperNE`, enabling formatted error output; `ASSERT_EQ` uses `ASSERT_PRED_FORMAT2` with `::testing::internal::EqHelper::Compare`.  
- Both ultimately compare values, but only `ASSERT_EQ` causes a crash (assertion failure) and stops execution.  
- `EXPECT_EQ` is suitable for production or non-critical tests; `ASSERT_EQ` is intended for internal debugging only.

tests.title: Explain absl flat_hash_map tradeoffs
tests.prompt: Explain flat_hash_map performance benefits and pointer-stability tradeoffs.
tests.response:
- **O(1) average-time operations**: Insertion, deletion, and lookup in `absl::flat_hash_map` run in average constant time (O(1)), similar to `std::unordered_map`, due to its hash-based indexing.  
- **Lower memory footprint**: `flat_hash_map` uses less memory than `std::unordered_map` because it stores pointers to entries in contiguous storage instead of allocating per-entry memory structures.  
- **Fixed capacity**: Unlike `std::unordered_map` which dynamically resizes, `flat_hash_map` has a fixed capacity determined at construction, preventing costly rehashing and improving cache locality.  
- **No exception safety**: `flat_hash_map` is **not** exception-safe; operations may corrupt the container on exception, requiring careful exception handling.  
- **Pointer-stable**: The internal memory layout is pointer-stable, meaning the pointers to entries are stable across operations, enabling efficient iteration and traversal.  
- **Compiler-friendly**: Since it's a fixed-size, contiguous container, it is often better suited for embedded and performance-critical environments.  
- **No rehashing overhead**: No dynamic resizing means no repeated hash computation or memory reallocation, reducing

tests.notes: Retrieval precheck failed: Expected repo token missing. Expected file fragment missing. Keyword hits 0 < 1.
tests.response:
- `absl::string_view` is an alias for `std::string_view` (C++17), meaning it shares ownership and lifetime constraints with `std::string_view`.
- `string_view` does not own the underlying character data; it only holds a pointer and length, with lifetime tied to the source string's lifetime.
- `ClippedSubstr()` returns a `string_view` derived from `s`, maintaining the same lifetime constraints as `s`.
- `NullSafeStringView()` (incomplete in snippet) creates a `string_view` from a null pointer, but such usage is unsafe and discouraged—likely intended to return an empty or zero-length view.
- Ownership remains with the source string; `string_view` is read-only and cannot modify the original data.
- Lifetimes are strictly coupled to the source object; dangling `string_view`s are undefined behavior.

tests.title: Explain folly FBVector growth strategy
tests.prompt: Explain FBVector growth behavior and reserve_in_place intent.
tests.notes: Retrieval precheck failed: Expected repo token missing. Expected file fragment missing. Keyword hits 0 < 1.
tests.response:
- `reserve_in_place` is a low-level memory allocation and relocation function used to grow the vector's underlying storage without reallocating.
- It attempts to increase the vector's capacity in situ by using `impl_.b_` as a buffer to relocate elements.
- If the current storage (`impl_.b_`) is non-empty and can accommodate more elements, it uses `reserve_in_place` to extend it.
- Otherwise, it allocates a larger chunk via `goodMallocSize` and `M_allocate`, relocates data, and updates the vector's internal pointers.
- The function avoids full allocation/deallocation, aiming for efficient growth when space is available.
- `reserve_in_place` is called internally to try and extend the vector when possible, minimizing memory churn.

`folly/AtomicHashmap.h`
----------------------

`folly/AtomicHashmap.h` introduces a synchronized UnorderedAssociativeContainer
implementation designed for extreme performance in heavily multithreaded
environments (about 2-5x faster than tbb::concurrent_hash_map) and good memory
usage properties.  Find and iteration are wait-free, insert has key-level lock
granularity, there is minimal memory overhead, and permanent 32-bit ids can be
used to reference each element.

### Implementation
***

AtomicHashMap is a composition of AtomicHashArray submaps, which implement the
meat of the functionality.  Only one AHA is created on initialization, and
additional submaps are appended if the first one gets full.  If the AHM grows,
there will be multiple submaps that must be probed in series to find a given
key.  The more growth, the more submaps will be chained, and the slower it will
get.  If the initial size estimate is good, only one submap will ever be created
and performance will be optimal.

AtomicHashArray is a fixed-size probing hash map (also referred to as an open
addressed hash map) where hash collisions are resolved by checking subsequent
elements.  This means that they can be allocated in slabs as arrays of
value_type elements, have excellent cache performance, and have no memory
overhead from storing pointers.

The algorithm is simple - when inserting, the key is hash-mod'ed to an offset,
and that element-key is atomically compare-and-swap'ed with the locked key
value.  If successful, the value is written and the element-key is unlocked by
setting it to the input key value.  If the compare fails, the next element is
tried until success or the map is full.

Finds are even simpler.  The key is hash-mod'ed to an offset, and the
element-key is examined.  If it is the same as the input key, the reference is
returned, if it's the empty key, failure is returned, otherwise the next key is
tried.  This can be done wait-free without any atomic instructions because the
elements are always in a valid state.

Erase is done by finding the key, then compare-and-swap'ing the element-key with
the reserved erased key value.  If the swap succeeds, return success, otherwise
return failure (the element was erased by a competing thread).  If the key do

tests.title: Optimize quick_sort partition recommendations
tests.prompt: Recommend concrete performance optimizations for this quick_sort implementation.
tests.response:
### Optimization Recommendations for `quick_sort.cpp` (Partition Function):

1. **Change Pivot Selection from Last Element to Median-of-Three**  
   - **Rationale**: Picking the last element as pivot can lead to worst-case O(n²) time on already sorted or reverse-sorted data. Median-of-three (comparing first, middle, last) improves pivot quality, reducing unbalanced partitions and improving average-case performance.

2. **Use In-Place Partitioning with Two Pointers (Hoare’s Partition)**  
   - **Rationale**: Current implementation likely uses nested loops or extra space. Hoare’s partition minimizes swaps and is efficient with O(n) time and O(1) extra space.

3. **Avoid std::sort in Final Sort**  
   - **Rationale**: If quick_sort is used to sort, compare its performance with `std::sort`. C++’s `std::sort` is highly optimized and often faster for general cases. Quick sort is preferable only when stability or specific pivot control is needed.

4. **In-place Element Swapping Only**  
   - **Rationale**: Minimize memory writes by using in-place swaps instead of copying elements to new vectors.

**Optimization Recommendations for `refer()` in LRU Cache:**

1. **Replace `pageMap.find()` with `pageMap.count()` for faster lookups**

   - *Rationale*: `Test` class is explicitly `nodiscard` and **not copyable** (`Test` is non-copyable). Creating multiple copies of test fixtures (e.g., in test suites) is unnecessary and can lead to dangling references if destroyed prematurely.

2. **Limit Scope of Shared State**  
   - *Rationale*: `Test` is shared across all tests in a suite; ensure setup/teardown methods (`SetUp`, `TearDown`) are **idempotent** and **reset shared state** to prevent cross-test contamination.  
   - *Fix*: Call `Reset()` or clear shared variables at the start of each test.

3. **Use `TestInfo` for Custom Test Discovery**  
   - *Rationale*: `TestInfo` enables custom test discovery without modifying test code, reducing coupling.  
   - *Fix*: Implement `TestInfo`

*Rationale:* The `rehash()` method can trigger O(n) reallocation. If rehashing is triggered more often than it should (e.g., due to aggressive `max_load_factor()` lowering), it degrades performance. Use `max_load_factor()` only to tune rehash thresholds, not to force frequent resizes.

### Limitations
***

Although it can provide extreme performance, AtomicHashmap has some unique
limitations as well.

* The space for erased elements cannot be reclaimed (they are tombstoned
forever) so it's generally not a good idea to use this if you're erasing things
a lot.

* Only supports 32 or 64 bit keys - this is because they must be atomically
compare-and-swap'ed.

* Growth beyond initialization reduces performance - if you don't know
the approximate number of elements you'll be inserting into the map, you
probably shouldn't use this class.

* Must manage synchronization externally in order to modify values in the map
after insertion.  Lock pools are a common way to do this, or you may
consider using `folly::PackedSyncPtr<T>` as your `ValueT`.

* Must define special reserved key values for empty, erased, and locked
elements.

For a complete list of limitations and departures from the
UnorderedAssociativeContainer concept, see `folly/AtomicHashMap.h`

### Optimization Recommendations for `AtomicHashMap`

1. **Reduce Reallocation Costs via Better Capacity Estimation**
   - *Rationale*: Linear performance degradation occurs due to frequent reallocation. Reallocating memory (e.g., via `std::vector` doubling) causes cache misses and temporary lock contention.
   - *Action*: Improve default capacity estimation using average key/value count * 1.2–1.5 (not 18x). For example, use `initial_capacity = std::min(1024, std::max(1, (keys + 1023)/1024 * 1.5))`.

   - *Rationale*: Max size ~18× initial capacity implies large arrays increase lock scope (per-cell locking), worsening contention.

We need to produce a unified diff that uses PauseTiming/ResumeTiming to isolate setup work from measured work. The code currently has BM_spin_pause_before which does setup (doNotOptimize) before measurement. We need to restructure: setup in a separate function and use PauseTiming/ResumeTiming.

We need to produce diff patch. Identify file: likely benchmark/basic_test.cc? The repository path: test/basic_test.cc. So we need to modify that file.

We need to extract the setup loop (doNotOptimize) before measurement into a separate function that is called before measurement but not part of measurement loop. Then measurement loop does something else (e.g., iterate). Use PauseTiming/ResumeTiming to wrap measurement.

In Google Benchmark, you can use:

benchmark::State& state; // already exists.

We can call benchmark::PauseTiming(state); before loop, then inside loop we do measurement (e.g., iterations), then resume.

But measurement is done by the state loop: for (auto _ : state) { ... } where you can do measurement inside.

PauseTiming/ResumeTiming are benchmark::PauseTiming(&state) and benchmark::ResumeTiming(&state)

 *       \mathrm{MdAPE}(e) = \mathrm{med}\{| \frac{e_i - \mathrm{med}\{e\}}{e_i}| \}

 *      error to this median for each measurement, as in  @f$ | \frac{e_i - \mathrm{med}\{e\}}{e_i}| @f$. All these results
 *      are sorted, and the middle value is chosen as the median absolute percent error.

 *      This measurement is a bit hard to interpret, but it is very robust against outliers. E.g. a value of 5% means that half of the
